name: differentiated instruction in math
creation_notes: Math-specific differentiation: readiness-based grouping, worked examples, manipulatives, visual models, task variation, number talks, and extension problems. Include examples for mixed readiness and productive struggle.

softfail_terms_found: saving teachers time (Key Takeaways — no explicit 'time for what' follow-through in that bullet)

seo_structure.title_tag_suggestion: Differentiated Instruction in Math: Strategies That Work
seo_structure.meta_description_suggestion: Practical strategies for differentiated instruction in math, from readiness-based grouping to manipulatives and extension tasks. Reach every learner in your classroom.

qapeds.gate1_reason: Title is declarative ('Differentiated Instruction in Math: Practical Strategies for Every Classroom'). No explicit interrogative question appears in the title or within the first 120 words. The article opens with a classroom scenario, not a question.
qapeds.gate2_reason: The second paragraph contains a functional definition ('Effective differentiated instruction in math doesn't mean creating entirely different lessons...') but it is not structurally isolated as a standalone answer block. It is embedded in narrative prose and requires the opening scenario for full context.

strengths: Excellent strategy reference table matching strategies to student needs with specific math examples, purpose, and tech tie-ins — highly scannable and practically useful

Strong math-domain expertise evident in accurate use of pedagogical terminology (productive struggle, number talks, algebra tiles) and specific, realistic problem examples (factoring x² + 5x + 6, graphing y = 2x + 3)
Well-structured FAQ section with six practical Q&A pairs addressing real teacher implementation concerns, providing strong FAQPage schema opportunity

recommendations.issue: Zero named citations or attributed sources. 'Studies in cognitive science reveal' is a generic attribution that actively damages credibility. No expert quotes, no institutional references, no research with author/year/source.
recommendations.fix: Add 3-5 named citations: (1) Carol Ann Tomlinson's differentiation framework (2001/2014), (2) NCTM's Principles to Actions (2014) on productive struggle, (3) Rosenshine's Principles of Instruction for worked examples, (4) Hiebert & Grouws (2007) on teaching for understanding vs. skill efficiency. Replace 'Studies in cognitive science reveal' with 'According to Hiebert and Grouws (2007), students who engage in productive struggle develop deeper conceptual understanding than those given fully worked procedures.'

recommendations.issue: No clean, structurally isolated direct answer block in the first 150 words. The opening is a scenario + framing narrative that AI systems cannot extract as a canonical definition/answer.
recommendations.fix: Add a standalone definition block immediately after the opening scenario (before the second paragraph): 'Differentiated instruction in math is the practice of varying support, representations, and challenge levels so every student works toward the same grade-level standards at their own readiness level. Teachers adjust tasks, grouping, and tools — not the learning goals — based on ongoing formative assessment.' Format this as a visually distinct block (callout, bold, or labeled 'Definition').

recommendations.issue: Statistical density is near zero. No percentages, no data points, no quantitative evidence anywhere in the article. This severely limits citation potential across all AI platforms.
recommendations.fix: Add 4-6 attributed statistics: (1) NCTM position statement data on heterogeneous grouping outcomes, (2) Specific effect sizes from Tomlinson's differentiation research, (3) Rosenshine's data on worked examples reducing cognitive load, (4) Any available data on manipulative use and math achievement (e.g., Carbonneau, Marley, & Selig, 2013 meta-analysis showing d = 0.50 for manipulative use). Place at least one stat in the first 200 words.

recommendations.issue: URL slug is not set. Meta description wastes 30+ characters on 'Try Wayground's tools today!' which is promotional and provides no search value.
recommendations.fix: Set slug to 'differentiated-instruction-in-math'. Revise meta description to: 'Practical strategies for differentiated instruction in math, from readiness-based grouping to manipulatives and extension tasks. Reach every learner in your classroom.' (160 chars, keyword-rich, value-focused, no sales pitch).

recommendations.issue: No first-person practitioner signals or first-party data. The Maria example is a nice touch but reads as hypothetical. No 'we tested,' 'based on feedback from X teachers,' or author credential signals.
recommendations.fix: Add 1-2 first-party authority signals: (1) 'Based on feedback from Wayground educators who use math differentiation tools daily...' (2) 'When we spoke with middle school math teachers about their biggest differentiation challenges...' Also ensure the published article has an author byline with credentials linking to an author page.

geo_narrative:
## GEO Evaluation: Differentiated Instruction in Math

### Weighted Executive Summary

This article demonstrates solid math-domain expertise and strong structural organization — particularly the strategy reference table and FAQ section — but is critically undermined by a complete absence of named citations, attributed data, or authoritative sources. With a partial GEO Composite of **40.9** (excluding brand voice) and a QAPEDS score of **4.15 (Weak)**, the article fails both QAPEDS gates and falls short of citation readiness across all five AI platforms. The core fix is adding 3-5 named research citations, a structurally isolated definition block, and quantitative evidence to transform this from a well-organized opinion piece into a citable authority resource.

---

### GEO Composite Score Breakdown

| Dimension | Score | Weight | Weighted Contribution |
|-----------|-------|--------|-----------------------|
| AI Citability | 45 | 25% | 11.25 |
| Content E-E-A-T | 44 | 20% | 8.80 |
| Platform Optimization | 39 | 20% | 7.80 |
| Brand Voice | _null_ | 15% | _(scored by Brand Reviewer)_ |
| SEO Structure | 52 | 10% | 5.20 |
| Schema Recommendations | 78 | 10% | 7.80 |
| **Partial Total** | | **85%** | **40.85** |

**Estimated full composite (if brand voice were scored):** ~48/100

---

### QAPEDS Score Breakdown

| Dimension | Score | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| **Q** (Question) | 4/10 | **Gate 1 FAIL.** Title is declarative. No interrogative sentence in first 120 words. The implied query ("how do I differentiate math instruction?") is never explicitly stated. Capped at ≤5. |
| **A** (Answer) | 4/10 | **Gate 2 FAIL.** Second paragraph contains a functional definition but it's embedded in narrative, not structurally isolated. Requires reading the opening scenario for context. Capped at ≤5. |
| **P** (Proof) | 2/10 | Zero named citations. "Studies in cognitive science reveal" is a textbook generic attribution — caps Evidence Presence at ≤1 per rubric. No expert quotes, no institutional sources, no data with year/author. This is the article's most critical weakness. |
| **E** (Expansion) | 7/10 | Strong logical layering: foundations → strategies → reference table → FAQ → tools. Good scannability via table and FAQ. Minor redundancy between "Foundations" and "Readiness-Based Grouping" sections (both cover same standards/different paths concept). |
| **D** (Decision) | 5/10 | Strategy table's "Works Best For" column provides useful decision guidance. FAQ addresses common scenarios. Missing: explicit trade-offs, when NOT to use a strategy, time investment comparisons, and implementation caveats. |
| **S** (Structure) | 4/10 | Good header hierarchy and table structure. Answer Isolation capped at ≤1 due to Gate 2 failure. Entities like "productive struggle" are used but not always explicitly defined on first use. Promotional conclusion adds noise. |

**QAPEDS Composite: 4.15 — Weak**

---

### Gate Failures

**Gate 1 — Explicit Question Gate: FAILED**
The article title "Differentiated Instruction in Math: Practical Strategies for Every Classroom" is declarative. The opening paragraph paints a classroom scenario but poses no explicit question. For AI systems that match queries to answers, there is no clear "question anchor" for the content to be cited against. The implied query exists but is never surfaced.

**Gate 2 — Direct Answer Gate: FAILED**
The closest thing to a direct answer appears in the second paragraph: "Effective differentiated instruction in math doesn't mean creating entirely different lessons for each student. Instead, you vary the support, representations, and challenge while keeping everyone moving toward the same standards." However, this is embedded in flowing prose, requires the opening scenario for full context, and is not formatted as a standalone extractable block (callout, definition box, or labeled answer).

---

### Per-Dimension Deep Dive

#### AI Citability: 45/100
The article's biggest citability asset is the strategy reference table — it's well-structured, specific, and self-contained enough for AI extraction. The FAQ answers are also reasonably citable. However, the lack of any answer block in the opening, zero statistical density, and the absence of named sources mean AI systems have little reason to prefer this article over competitors. The "Maria" example is a nice uniqueness touch but reads as hypothetical rather than documented.

#### Content E-E-A-T: 44/100
Math-domain expertise is genuinely strong — the article uses pedagogical terminology correctly (productive struggle, number talks, algebra tiles) and provides mathematically accurate examples. However, authoritativeness is critically weak at 20/100. "Studies in cognitive science reveal" without any named source is worse than no citation at all, as it signals either AI generation or academic laziness. No expert quotes, no institutional backing (NCTM, What Works Clearinghouse), and no author credentials visible.

#### Platform Optimization: 39/100
No platform flags are met. Google AIO requires a direct answer block + named citation — this article has neither in extractable form. ChatGPT needs factual density and clear content-answer fit — the title-to-content alignment is decent but factual density is near zero. Perplexity requires ≥2 named stats per 500 words — the article has zero across ~1,400 words. The FAQ section is the article's best platform asset but cannot compensate for absent authority signals.

#### SEO Structure: 52/100
The heading hierarchy is well-organized and keyword placement is natural and appropriate. However, the URL slug is missing entirely (scored 0), the meta description wastes characters on a promotional CTA ("Try Wayground's tools today!"), and the title tag at 61 characters slightly exceeds the 50-60 character target. These are straightforward fixes that would significantly improve this score.

#### Schema Recommendations: 78/100
The article clearly supports three schema types: Article (standard), FAQPage (six well-formed Q&A pairs), and LearningResource (educational content for teachers). The table could potentially support additional structured data. Schema implementation would be straightforward given the current structure.

---

### Top 5 Prioritized Recommendations

#### 1. Add 3-5 Named Research Citations (Authority — E-E-A-T + AI Citability)
**Issue:** Zero named sources. "Studies in cognitive science reveal" is a generic attribution that per the rubric caps Evidence Presence at ≤1.
**Fix:** Add citations to: Carol Ann Tomlinson (2001/2014) on differentiation framework, NCTM's *Principles to Actions* (2014) on productive struggle, Rosenshine's Principles of Instruction for worked examples, and Carbonneau, Marley & Selig (2013) meta-analysis on manipulative use. Format as "According to [Source, Year]" per brand guide.

#### 2. Add a Structurally Isolated Definition/Answer Block (Structural — AI Citability)
**Issue:** No extractable answer block in the first 150 words. AI systems cannot identify a canonical answer to cite.
**Fix:** Insert a standalone 40-60 word definition block after the opening scenario: "Differentiated instruction in math is the practice of varying support, representations, and challenge levels so every student works toward the same grade-level standards at their own readiness level. Teachers adjust tasks, grouping, and tools — not the learning goals — based on ongoing formative assessment."

#### 3. Add Quantitative Evidence With Attribution (Authority — AI Citability + Platform Optimization)
**Issue:** Zero statistics, percentages, or quantitative data in the entire article. This eliminates citation potential on Perplexity and severely limits other platforms.
**Fix:** Add 4-6 attributed data points distributed across the article. Place at least one in the first 200 words. Examples: effect sizes from differentiation research, NAEP data on math achievement gaps, or meta-analysis results on manipulative effectiveness.

#### 4. Set URL Slug and Fix Meta Description (Formatting — SEO Structure)
**Issue:** No URL slug exists. Meta description uses 30+ characters on "Try Wayground's tools today!" — promotional text that provides no search or citation value.
**Fix:** Set slug to `differentiated-instruction-in-math`. Revise meta to: "Practical strategies for differentiated instruction in math, from readiness-based grouping to manipulatives and extension tasks. Reach every learner in your classroom."

#### 5. Add First-Party Authority Signals (Content — E-E-A-T)
**Issue:** No practitioner voice, no first-party data signals. The Maria example reads as hypothetical. No author credentials or "we tested/observed" language.
**Fix:** Add 1-2 signals: "Based on feedback from Wayground educators who use math differentiation tools daily..." or "When we interviewed middle school math teachers about their differentiation challenges..." Ensure published version includes author byline with credentials.

---

### Pre-Publish Checklist

| Item | Recommendation |
|------|---------------|
| **Schema to Add** | `Article`, `FAQPage`, `LearningResource` |
| **Title Tag** | "Differentiated Instruction in Math: Strategies That Work" (55 chars) |
| **Meta Description** | "Practical strategies for differentiated instruction in math, from readiness-based grouping to manipulatives and extension tasks. Reach every learner in your classroom." (160 chars) |
| **URL Slug** | `differentiated-instruction-in-math` |
brand_narrative:
I'll systematically audit this article against every rule in the Wayground Brand Voice Guide.

**Pre-audit checklist:**

**A. Naming compliance:**
- "Wayground" used twice in body — correct name used
- No "Quizizz" found
- No "quiz tool," "quiz app," "quiz maker," or "quiz platform" found
- "Supplemental" not used — checking if needed

**B. Em dashes:**
- Scanning... "This readiness range isn't a problem to solve. It's the reality" — fine
- No em dashes (—) found in the body text. PASS.

**C. Heading case:**
- "Foundations: Why Differentiated Instruction in Math Works" — "Why," "Differentiated," "Instruction," "Math," "Works" all capitalized → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Productive Struggle Builds Mathematical Thinking" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Same Standards, Multiple Learning Pathways" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Responsive Grouping Through Quick Check-Ins" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Readiness-Based Grouping and Task Variation That Keep Standards Shared" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Quick Reference: Matching Strategies to Student Needs" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- "Differentiated Instruction in Math: Frequently Asked Questions" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL
- All FAQ sub-headings: "How can teachers use differentiated instruction in math to support students with varying readiness levels?" — these open with questions → HARD FAIL (headings open with questions)
- "What's the most efficient way to form readiness-based groups without extensive prep time?" → HARD FAIL (heading opens with question)
- "How do manipulatives and visual tools enhance learning across different readiness levels?" → HARD FAIL
- "How can I differentiate math instruction when teaching remotely or with limited technology?" → HARD FAIL
- "What if some learners finish differentiated tasks much faster than others?" → HARD FAIL
- "How do I assess student progress in a differentiated math classroom?" → HARD FAIL
- "Bring Differentiation to Life With Time-Saving Tools" → TITLE CASE → HARD FAIL

**D. Article opening:**
- First sentence: "Walk into any Algebra I classroom, and you'll find students spanning pre-algebra gaps to honors-level curiosity." — NOT a question, NOT a product mention. PASS.

**E. HQIM positioning:** No claim that Wayground is core curriculum. PASS.

**F. AI framing:** No AI-replaces-teacher claim. PASS.

**Vocabulary substitutions:**
- "Kids" — not found
- "ELL" — not found
- "Modifications" — not found
- "Users/customers" — not found
- "Slideshow" — not found
- "Reading handout" — not found
- "Quiz tool/app" — not found
- "SPED" — not found
- "Just" or "simply" — scanning... not found
- "Saves time" standalone: "Technology tools streamline differentiation, saving teachers time while supporting all learners' progress." — "saving teachers time" appears without "time for what?" directly. This is in the Key Takeaways. Soft fail.
- "Saves time" in heading: "Making Math Work for Everyone: Differentiation Strategies That Save Time" — this is an alternative title, not published body. Still flag.
- "Free you to focus on what matters most: teaching." — this addresses "time for what" adequately. The Key Takeaways version is less fully resolved.
- "Revolutionary," "game-changing" — not found
- "Should," "must" — scanning... "Both allow learners to explain their reasoning and make connections." — fine. Not found in prescriptive sense.
- "Learners" vs "students" — "learners" used throughout as a substitute for "students" — this is acceptable per brand voice, not a violation.
- "Person" used instead of "student": "one person might need graphing paper" — this is slightly unusual but not a rule violation.

**Product mention count:**
- "Wayground offers step-by-step presentations and built-in accommodations that might help streamline your differentiation work." (body paragraph 2)
- "Wayground's step-by-step presentations and Desmos integration make it easy to assign different pathways" (FAQ answer)
- "Check out Wayground today and start supporting every student's mathematical journey." (conclusion)
- Count: 3 mentions — PASS

**Product accuracy:**
- "built-in accommodations" — accurate, Wayground has 25+ accommodations
- "Wayground's step-by-step presentations and Desmos integration" — Desmos Calculator is listed as an accommodation/integration, this seems accurate
- "step-by-step presentations" — this could refer to Lesson/Presentation format with AI-Powered Answer Explanations; acceptable
- "mastery-focused assessments" — references Mastery Mode, acceptable
- No specific inaccurate claims found

**Tone checks:**
- Opens with concrete classroom scenario — POSITIVE
- "might help streamline" — slightly hedging but acceptable
- AI content indicators: no "In today's fast-paced world" opener; no "It is important to note" — PASS
- Concrete classroom examples throughout — POSITIVE
- Benefits-led framing — POSITIVE
- "Check out Wayground today" — slightly promotional/CTA-forward but acceptable for conclusion

**Heading case violations — detailed:**
ALL headings are in title case. This is a systematic HARD FAIL.

**FAQ headings open with questions — detailed:**
Per the rule: "NEVER open any heading with a question — headings state, they don't ask." All six FAQ sub-headings are questions. This compounds the heading HARD FAIL.

**Intro word count:**
"Walk into any Algebra I classroom, and you'll find students spanning pre-algebra gaps to honors-level curiosity. Some need concrete manipulatives to grasp slope, while others are ready to explore transformations. This readiness range isn't a problem to solve. It's the reality that makes differentiated instruction in math both necessary and powerful." — approximately 60 words. PASS (60-80 target).

**H2 opener paragraph length:**
- Under "Foundations: Why Differentiated Instruction in Math Works": "Picture your Algebra I class: some learners still struggle with integer operations, while others are ready for quadratic extensions. Studies in cognitive science reveal that effective math differentiation strategies work because they honor these readiness differences while keeping everyone moving toward shared standards. The key is varying the support, not the destination." — ~55 words. Borderline but within 40... actually 55 words exceeds 40. Soft flag.
- Under "Readiness-Based Grouping...": "The beauty of readiness-based grouping in math lies in its flexibility. Instead of placing students in fixed ability groups, you can use quick snapshots of understanding to create responsive learning experiences that meet everyone where they are while moving toward shared goals." — ~46 words. Over 40. Soft flag.
- Under "Bring Differentiation to Life With Time-Saving Tools": "Differentiated math instruction doesn't have to mean triple the prep time. When you can assign access, on-level, and extension variations with a few clicks and automatically enable built-in accommodations, responsive grouping becomes a seamless part of your routine. The right math differentiation tools free you to focus on what matters most: teaching." — ~53 words. Over 40. Soft flag.

**Oxford commas:**
- "...instructional hub — instruction + practice + assessment in one place" — not applicable here
- "Provide worked examples for those building foundational skills, graphic organizers for independent practice, and open-ended extensions for ready-for-challenge learners." — Oxford comma present ✓
- "Step-by-step presentations, Desmos graphing integration, and mastery-focused assessments" — Oxford comma present ✓
- Generally consistent. PASS.

**Scoring:**

Start: 100

Hard Fail violations:
- Title-case headings (systematic — counting as ONE hard fail rule violated): -20
- FAQ headings open with questions (NEVER open any heading with a question — this is a separate hard fail rule): -20

Vocabulary:
- "saving teachers time" without clear "time for what" in Key Takeaways: -3 (soft fail, tone/style)
- No other vocabulary violations

Tone/style:
- H2 opener paragraphs over 40 words (3 instances): -3 × 3 = -9 (tone/style, -3 each)
- "saving teachers time" key takeaway: already counted above

Total deductions: -20 -20 -9 -3 = -52

Score: 100 - 52 = 48

Verdict: FAIL (hard fail violations present AND score < 50)

<output>

## Brand Verdict: FAIL — Score 48/100

Two hard fail violations are present, both structural and systematic. Neither involves a single slip — they affect every heading in the article. The good news is these are mechanical fixes that don't require rewriting any substantive content.

---

## Hard fail issues

### 1. All headings are in title case — HARD FAIL (−20 pts)

Every H2 and H3 in this article uses title case. Wayground brand rules require **sentence case always** — capitalize only the first word and proper nouns/product names.

**Every heading needs to be corrected.** Here is the full list with fixes:

| Current (WRONG) | Corrected (sentence case) |
|---|---|
| Foundations: Why Differentiated Instruction in Math Works | Foundations: why differentiated instruction in math works |
| Productive Struggle Builds Mathematical Thinking | Productive struggle builds mathematical thinking |
| Same Standards, Multiple Learning Pathways | Same standards, multiple learning pathways |
| Responsive Grouping Through Quick Check-Ins | Responsive grouping through quick check-ins |
| Readiness-Based Grouping and Task Variation That Keep Standards Shared | Readiness-based grouping and task variation that keep standards shared |
| Quick Reference: Matching Strategies to Student Needs | Quick reference: matching strategies to student needs |
| Differentiated Instruction in Math: Frequently Asked Questions | Differentiated instruction in math: frequently asked questions |
| Bring Differentiation to Life With Time-Saving Tools | Bring differentiation to life with time-saving tools |

Note: "Algebra I," "Wayground," and "Desmos" remain capitalized as proper nouns.

---

### 2. Six FAQ subheadings open with questions — HARD FAIL (−20 pts)

The rule is unambiguous: **headings state, they never ask.** All six FAQ subheadings are interrogative sentences. This compounds the title-case violation above, and both must be fixed before the article can publish.

Suggested rewrites (also correcting to sentence case):

| Current (WRONG) | Corrected |
|---|---|
